sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 39-year-old male, presents with a 3-month history of intermittent cough, initially noticed while loading horses, which was exacerbated by a cold and diagnosed as bronchitis by his primary care physician.
A chest X-ray showed a lung nodule, which was confirmed by a CT scan.
He reports being a former smoker, quitting 7 years ago, and has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, well-controlled with methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, well-controlled with methotrexate 7.5mg weekly, and a history of smoking, quitting 7 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient denies pain, shortness of breath, or any other symptoms.
Current medications: Methotrexate 7.5mg weekly for rheumatoid arthritis.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/76, heart rate 70, respiration rate 16, O2 sat on room air 98%.
Physical exam: The lungs are clear and equal bilateral, the heart has a regular rate and rhythm with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs, and the extremities have intact pulses and brisk capillary refill.
The skin is pink, warm, and dry with slight swelling in the knuckles, which is consistent with rheumatoid arthritis.
Test Results: A chest CT scan shows a solitary 2cm nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, smooth in appearance, with no evidence of emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule that warrants further evaluation.
Problem list: Lung nodule; Rheumatoid Arthritis; History of smoking.
Plan: I will order a pulmonary function test (PFT) to assess lung function, and schedule a PET CT to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.
I will also schedule a video-assisted thoracoscopy to remove the nodule.
The patient will be under general anesthesia for approximately 1.5 hours, and will have a chest tube until the following day.
They will be admitted for 1 night and discharged the next day.
Follow-up: The patient will be discharged the day after the procedure, and will follow up with their rheumatologist for continued management of rheumatoid arthritis.
